segments.s692-g25: بر here is a preposition meaning 'to/toward' and introduces the indirect object 'خسرو' (to Khosrow). The past verb آمد (came) appears before its subject — a poetic/alternative word order — so فرستاده مرد is the subject phrase 'a messenger, a man' (noun + appositive) following the verb for emphasis.
segments.s692-g26: سخنهای قیصر is a genitive noun phrase 'the words/speeches of the Caesar' (سخنهای = 'words/speeches' + plural/partitive), with همه ('all') quantifying the phrase. یاد کرد is a compound of the noun یاد with the past auxiliary کرد, here functioning as a past tense verbal phrase 'recounted/remembered/mentioned' referring back to those words.
segments.s692-g1: دبیر جهاندیده را پیش خواند = object (دبیر جهاندیده) is marked by را and placed before the verb; خواند (from خواندن) here means 'to call/summon'. پیش is an adverb/prepositional element 'before/in the presence of' and together with خواند yields 'called forward into the presence'. The sequence is object+adverb+verb typical of Persian poetry.
segments.s692-g2: بران پیشگاه بزرگی نشاند = بران is a poetic contraction of بر آن ('on/at that'); پیشگاه بزرگی = 'that great presence/court'. نشاند is the archaic/transitive past of نشاندن 'to seat/place (someone)'. Together: 'he seated him at/on that great court.'
segments.s692-g3: بفرمود تا نامه پاسخ نوشت = بفرمود is an honorific/polite past 'he commanded/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/command ('that/so that'); نامه پاسخ 'a reply‑letter' is the object, and نوشت here is the verb 'to write' used in classical Persian as the content of the command ('that (someone) write').
segments.s692-g4: بیاراست چون مرغزار بهشت = بیاراست is a causative/active verb 'he adorned' or 'had (it) adorned'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as', and مرغزار بهشت is the comparative image 'a meadow of paradise'. The clause modifies the letter (i.e., he had it beautifully prepared).
segments.s692-g5: ز بس بند و پیوند و نیکو سخن = ز (poetic 'from/because of') + بس ('so many' / 'such abundance' here) introduces a list of poetic elements. بند is used in the poetic sense 'stanza' (not 'fetter'), پیوند 'links/joins' and نیکو سخن 'good/fine speech' all enumerate the composition's qualities.
segments.s692-g6: ازان روز تا روزگار کهن = ازان ('from that') sets the starting point; تا 'until' marks the end point; روز here functions as 'time/era' and روزگار کهن means 'the ancient/olden time'. The whole phrase gives a temporal span 'from that day to the olden days.'
segments.s692-g7: چوگشت از نوشتن نویسنده سیر = چوگشت is 'when it/he became' (چو + گشت); از + verbal noun نوشتن expresses cause/source ('weary from/of writing'); نویسنده is the agent 'the scribe' and سیر is a predicate adjective 'sated/weary'. The clause means 'when the scribe became tired/sated of writing.'
segments.s692-g8: نگه کرد قیصر سواری دلیر = نگه (a noun 'gaze/watch') + کرد forms the verbal expression 'cast a look/kept watch'. Word order is inverted in poetry: verb first, then subject with apposition (قیصر followed by سواری دلیر 'a valiant rider'), so 'the Caesar, a valiant horseman, looked/gazed.'
segments.s692-g17: بدان is the imperative 'know' formed from the prefix ب- + دان; تا introduces a temporal/clausal subordinate (here 'when/that'); تواز is the contracted sequence تو از ('you from') so the phrase reads 'when you from Rome...'; با کام خویش is a prepositional phrase 'with (your) fulfilled desire' where کام خویش is a noun + possessive suffix (خوی + ش). Word order is verb (imperative) + subordinate temporal clause.
segments.s692-g18: به ایران is the goal phrase 'into/ to Iran' placed before the verb; گذشتی is 2nd-person singular past 'you passed/came'; به آرام خویش is an adverbial phrase 'in your own calm/ease' where به functions as 'in' and خویش is the possessive (خوی + ش). Poetic order places the verb after its object phrases.
segments.s692-g19: مباش is the negative imperative 'do not be.' اندرین بوم is a locative phrase 'in this land' (اندرین = 'in this'); تیره روان is an adjectival phrase ('dark/ominous' + 'soul/spirit') describing the land's fortune or spirit. Adjective precedes noun in this poetic phrasing.
segments.s692-g20: چنین است is a formulaic 'such is' (چنین 'thus/so' + est 'is'); کردار چرخ روان is a nominal phrase 'the action/way of the wheel of fate' where چرخ روان ('the wheel that moves') is a compound describing the wheel as moving/fateful and stands as the logical subject whose behaviour is summarized.
segments.s692-g21: که introduces an explanatory relative clause ('that' / 'namely'); گاهی پناهست is 'sometimes it is a refuge' where پناهست is an older fused form 'is a refuge' (پناه + -ست). و گاهی گزند continues the alternation 'and sometimes (it is) harm.' The subject is implicit (the 'wheel' / fate mentioned before).
segments.s692-g22: گهی 'sometimes' begins the parallel: با زیانیم literally 'with loss to us' (زیانیم = زیان with an enclitic sense), and گه سودمند 'and sometimes beneficial' completes the antithesis. The construction contrasts opposite outcomes of the 'wheel.'
segments.s692-g23: کنون 'now' + تا introduces a purpose clause here ('so that / in order that'); سلیح و سپاه و درم is a simple coordinated list of direct objects 'weapons and an army and money' which will be the things to be brought by the speaker.
segments.s692-g24: فراز آورم is the verb of the purpose clause: آورم (1st-singular) often here expresses 'that I bring/raise up' and فراز adds the sense 'up/on high' or 'forth'; تو نباشی دژم is the admonition addressed to 'you' — نباشی functions as negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not be' and دژم is the predicative adjective 'sullen/grim.'
segments.s692-g9: This string of epithets is a nominal phrase with adjectives and participial descriptions chained by و (and). سخن گوی is a compound meaning ‘speaker’ or ‘one who speaks’ (literally ‘speech‑sayer’); روشن‌دل and یادگیر are adjectival/descriptive nouns placed after it and linked by و. There is no finite verb here; the phrase lists qualities of the person described in the surrounding narrative.
segments.s692-g10: Continuation of the adjectival list. خردمند and گویا are adjectives/nouns describing mental qualities (wise; eloquent), گرد here functions as a verbal/adjectival root meaning ‘one who goes about’ or ‘itinerant’, and دبیر is a noun ‘scribe/secretary’. The repeated و simply links each quality; the whole segment remains a noun phrase without an expressed verb.
segments.s692-g11: بدو گفت = ‘he said to him’ / ‘he told him’: بدو is an indirect object (‘to him’ or ‘unto him’) and گفت is the past stem of گفتن ‘said’. رو پیش (lit. ‘turn toward before’) functions as an instruction to go into the presence of someone; پیش خسرو = ‘before Khosrow’. بگوی is the imperative/subjunctive of گفتن used to introduce the quoted speech (‘tell’/‘say’). The word order is archaic/poetic but standard in Ferdowsi.
segments.s692-g12: This is the content of the quotation introduced by که: که here links reported speech, and ای (vocative) addresses the king directly: 'ای شاه' = 'O king'. بینا دل is a reversed adjectival phrase meaning ‘discerning in heart’ (بینا = discerning/clear‑sighted; دل = heart/mind). راه جوی is a compound meaning ‘one who seeks the way’ (جوی = seeker/search). Together they form the vocative phrase used in direct address.
segments.s692-g13: مرا here functions as the dative/experiencer ‘to me’ (i.e. ‘I have’), with an implied existential construction; را is the object/topic marker attached in the older syntax. هم ... هم ... links two possessed items: سلیحست (سلیح + است) = ‘there is weapon/armour’ (i.e. ‘I have arms’) and هم زر = ‘also gold’. به گنج indicates location or destination ‘in/into the treasury’. This is a compact way of saying ‘I am equipped and possess gold in the treasure.’
segments.s692-g14: This clause states a general principle with an archaic word order: نیاورد (negative perfective/passive sense ‘is not brought/obtained’) and باید (‘it is necessary’/'one must') together yield the sense ‘it must not be/get done without...’ or more idiomatically ‘...is not obtained without...’. کسی را (the indefinite someone, marked by را) is the beneficiary/experiencer. به رنج = ‘by toil/hardship’. Put together: ‘Treasure is not brought to anyone except through hardship’ (i.e. ‘one must toil for treasure’).
segments.s692-g15: وگر = ‘if’ (conditional); نیستیمان is the plural existential/negative (‘we are not’ / ‘we lack’) with the enclitic plural/possessive — here meaning ‘if we are lacking’. ز هر کشوری = ‘from/of every country/province’ (ز = from; هر = every; کشوری = of the country/region). The clause sets a conditional background: ‘And if we are lacking in any province…’
segments.s692-g16: درم = ‘a dirham’ (coin) and خواستیمی is an archaic 1st‑person plural past/present form meaning ‘we requested/asked for’ (here: ‘we have sought/asked’). ز هر مهتری = ‘from every chief/elder’ (ز = from; هر = every; مهتری = chief/lord). The clause says they have solicited small payments from every lord when lacking resources.

sentences.s692-s13: A messenger came to Khosrow and recounted all the words of the Caesar.
sentences.s692-s1: He summoned the worldly‑wise scribe forward and seated him before that great court.
sentences.s692-s2: He ordered that a letter of reply be written and had it adorned like a meadow of paradise.
sentences.s692-s3: With so many stanzas and links and fine words, from that day down to the olden time.
sentences.s692-s4: When the scribe grew weary of writing, the Caesar — a valiant horseman — cast his gaze.
sentences.s692-s9: Know this: when you left Byzantium having had your desire, you came into Iran in your own ease.
sentences.s692-s10: Do not be gloomy in this land of dark spirits; such is the behaviour of the moving wheel (i.e. fortune).
sentences.s692-s11: That is: sometimes it is a refuge and sometimes it (is) harm; sometimes it brings us loss and sometimes profit.
sentences.s692-s12: Now, so that I may bring up weapons, an army and money, do not be gloomy.
sentences.s692-s5: An eloquent speaker, bright‑minded and quick to learn, wise and eloquent, itinerant and a scribe.
sentences.s692-s6: He said to him, “Go and stand before Khosrow and tell him: ‘O king, discerning of heart and seeker of the way.’”
sentences.s692-s7: I have both weapons and also gold in the treasury; treasure is not acquired for anyone without toil.
sentences.s692-s8: And if we lack (wealth) in any province, we have asked a dirham from every lord.
